perturbation.original_text: CUSTOMER AGREES TO PAY FOR HOSTING SERVICES ON OR BEFORE THE FIRST DAY OF THE MONTH IN WHICH THE HOSTING SERVICES ARE PROVIDED, except that, with respect to Additional Fees (as defined in Exhibit A), eGain will invoice Customer for such Fees in the month after the month in which such fees accrue as provided in Exhibit A.
perturbation.changed_text: CUSTOMER AGREES TO PAY FOR HOSTING SERVICES ON OR BEFORE THE LAST DAY OF THE MONTH IN WHICH THE HOSTING SERVICES ARE PROVIDED, except that, with respect to Additional Fees (as defined in Exhibit A), eGain will invoice Customer for such Fees three months after the month in which such fees accrue as provided in Exhibit A.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to the last day of the month and invoicing three months late may violate California's prompt payment laws if they unreasonably delay payment, which can be interpreted as a breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3302 states that the detriment caused by the breach of an obligation to pay money only, is deemed to be the amount due by the terms of the obligation, with interest thereon. Delaying invoicing by three months and moving the payment date to the end of the month could be considered a breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing if it causes significant financial harm to the service provider, as businesses are generally expected to adhere to standard billing cycles unless otherwise explicitly agreed upon under reasonable terms. While it does not explicitly say you must invoice immediately, the implication is 'reasonable' time and standards.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. COMPENSATORY RELIEF [3281 - 3361] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Measure of Damages [[3300.] - 3361] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 1. Damages for Breach of Contract [[3300.] - 3322] ( Article 1 enacted 1872. ) 3302. The detriment caused by the breach of an obligation to pay money only, is deemed to be the amount due by the terms of the obligation, with interest thereon. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a legal contradiction by altering the payment terms to the last day of the month and delaying invoicing by three months. This contradicts the principle outlined in California Civil Code Section 3302, which states that the detriment caused by a breach of an obligation to pay money is the amount due with interest. While the law doesn't explicitly mandate immediate invoicing, the delayed payment and invoicing could be interpreted as a breach if it causes financial harm, as it deviates from reasonable and standard billing practices, making the obligation to pay more vague and allowing the customer to withhold funds for longer.

perturbation.original_text: Payment received by eGain after the due date shall be subject to a late fee equal to one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, or, if less, the maximum amount allowed by applicable law.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment received by eGain after the due date shall be subject to a late fee equal to twenty percent (20%) per month.
perturbation.explanation: Charging a late fee of 20% per month likely violates California usury laws, which limit the maximum interest rate that can be charged on a loan or forbearance of money.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article XV Section 1 of the California Constitution limits the interest rate for loans and forbearances. While the precise limit depends on the specific type of transaction and lender, a 20% monthly interest rate (240% annually) would almost certainly exceed the allowable rate, particularly since this is not a highly regulated lending industry. This constitutes usury under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The changed text increases the late fee to 20% per month, which likely violates California's usury laws as described in the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from the California Constitution, Article XV, Section 1, states that the interest rate on loans or forbearances shall be 7 percent per annum, but allows for written contracts to stipulate higher rates, with specific limits depending on the loan's purpose. The Law Explanation supports that a 20% monthly interest rate (240% annually) almost certainly exceeds the allowable rate. Therefore, the modified contract text creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating usury laws, thus making the 'YES' assignment appropriate.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall continue in effect from the Effective Date for a one (1) year period, unless earlier terminated as set forth below, and thereafter shall renew automatically for successive one (1) year periods unless either party gives the other party at least thirty (30) days prior written notice of its intent not to renew the Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall continue in effect from the Effective Date for a one (1) year period, unless earlier terminated as set forth below, and thereafter shall renew automatically for successive one (1) year periods unless either party gives the other party at least five (5) days prior written notice of its intent not to renew the Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-renewal notice period to 5 days from 30 days could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable, especially if it creates an undue burden or surprise on the customer, particularly if the hosting agreement is crucial for the customer's business operations. This would violate general principles of contract law requiring fair and reasonable terms, as interpreted by California courts.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1670.5 addresses the issue of unconscionable contracts or clauses. While it doesn't specify exact notice periods, courts can refuse to enforce a clause if it is found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. A five-day notice period for automatic renewal, especially in a critical service agreement like hosting, might be considered procedurally and substantively unconscionable because the client does not have much time to react.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for non-renewal from 30 days to 5 days, which could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable, potentially violating principles of fair and reasonable contract terms. This contradicts California Civil Code Section 1670.5, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. The reduced notice period might be considered unconscionable, especially if it creates an undue burden. Therefore, this creates a legal contradiction.
